cues.x: How to Win Friends and Influence People, by Dale Carnegie.
Here's a fact worth sitting with before we even start.
This book came out in 1936, in the middle of the Great Depression, and it has sold more than thirty million copies since then.
It has never once gone out of print, not for a single year, in nearly ninety years.
And here's the part that matters most for you, listening to this in your car or at the gym.

If you've gone through any of the other people skills or negotiation books in this library, Robert Cialdini's Influence, Chris Voss's Never Split the Difference, really any modern book that promises to teach you how to talk to another human being so they actually want to help you, you have already been drinking from a well that Dale Carnegie dug.
Cialdini's chapter on liking, the idea that we say yes to people we know and feel similar to, that's Carnegie.
Voss's whole obsession with letting the other side feel like an idea was theirs, that's Carnegie, decades earlier, stated just as plainly.
This is, in a very real sense, the root of the tree.
Almost every people skills book you will ever read is a branch off this trunk, whether the author admits it or not.
So today we are not doing the postcard version.
We're going deep, the way the original book goes deep, story by story, name by name, because Carnegie didn't build this out of theory.
He built it out of real people.
A killer who called himself kindhearted while he bled out behind a barricade of tear gas.
A steel executive who got paid a fortune mostly for how he made people feel.
A little boy's rabbit hutch that quietly taught a future titan of industry the most important lesson of his career.
Let's get into it.
But first you need to know where this book actually came from, because it explains why it still works today.
Dale Carnegie was not a psychologist.
He never ran a laboratory or earned a doctorate.
He grew up dirt poor on a Missouri farm, tried a few careers, took a swing at acting, and failed.
By his mid twenties he was broke, living in a cheap hotel in New York City, selling trucks for a living and hating nearly every minute of it.
What he actually wanted to do was teach.

So in 1912 he talked his way into the YMCA on 125th Street in Harlem and convinced the director to let him teach a night class in public speaking, not for a salary, but for a cut of whatever the students paid him directly.
He had no textbook, because there wasn't one yet.
Nobody had written the book he needed.
So most nights he was improvising an hour or two ahead of his own students.
And one night, by his own telling, he simply ran out of material halfway through class.
Standing there with nothing prepared and a room full of tired adults staring back at him, he did the only thing he could think of.
He asked the man in the front row to stand up and talk, off the cuff, about something that had actually made him angry recently.
The man did.
And it worked.
The room came alive.
People who froze up trying to recite a memorized speech turned out to be perfectly capable of talking with real feeling about something true.

Carnegie had stumbled onto something enormous by accident, that people don't need a script, they need permission to be honest, plus a little coaching on how to handle each other while they're being honest.

He kept teaching that way for the next two decades, first in New York, then across the country, to salesmen and engineers and executives, and every single time a technique actually worked in that room, he wrote it down on an index card.
By the mid 1930s he had file cabinets stuffed with these cards, real stories from real students about what actually got a stubborn boss to change his mind or a furious customer to calm down.
A publishing editor sat in on one of his talks, was floored by the material, and pushed him to turn it into a book.
Carnegie essentially emptied the file cabinets onto the page.
That's why this book doesn't read like a lecture.
It reads like a few hundred people telling you, one at a time, what actually happened to them.
So let's meet those people.
Part one of the book is called the fundamental techniques in handling people, and the very first technique, the one Carnegie places before absolutely everything else, is this.
Don't criticize, don't condemn, and don't complain.
That sounds almost too simple to be a foundational principle, so watch how he proves it's actually the hardest one of all.
He opens with two of the most wanted criminals in American history.
The first is Francis Crowley, known to the newspapers as Two Gun Crowley, a cop killer finally cornered by police in a rented apartment on West End Avenue in Manhattan in the spring of 1931.
A hundred and fifty officers surrounded that building.

They fired tear gas through the windows, mounted machine guns on the rooftops across the street, and for over an hour gunfire echoed up and down the block while ten thousand people crowded the sidewalks below to watch, the biggest siege New York had seen.

And while he was cornered and bleeding, waiting to either die or be dragged out in handcuffs, Crowley found a moment to write a note describing what was, in his own words, a weary heart, but a kind one, underneath his coat.

A man who had shot a police officer for no reason beyond a routine traffic stop genuinely believed, right up to the edge of his own death, that he was fundamentally a decent person being hunted unfairly.
Later, arriving at Sing Sing to be executed, his attitude was still that he was only being punished for defending himself.
He never once, in his own mind, did anything wrong.
You might think, sure, but that's a psychopath, that doesn't tell us much about ordinary people.
So Carnegie hands you a second example, and this one is more unsettling because the man was, by most accounts, genuinely charming.

Al Capone, the most notorious gangster of the era, described himself as someone who had spent his best years giving people a little harmless fun, only to be treated like a hunted animal for the trouble.
Capone did not see himself as a criminal.
He saw himself as a public benefactor, an entertainer, a guy providing a service that uptight lawmakers wouldn't allow.
Two of the worst men of their generation, and neither one thought of himself, even for a second, as the villain of his own story.
Here's Carnegie's actual point, and it isn't really about criminals at all.
It's about your spouse, your kid, your coworker, the guy who cut you off in traffic twenty minutes ago.
Ninety nine times out of a hundred, people don't criticize themselves for anything, no matter how wrong they clearly are.
Everybody is the hero of their own story, quietly explaining away their own worst behavior, the same way Crowley and Capone did, just at much lower stakes.
Which means that when you criticize someone, you're not delivering some clarifying truth that will snap them into self awareness.
You're attacking the story they already tell themselves about who they are.
And people don't reward that with gratitude.
They reward it with resentment and a hardening of the exact behavior you were trying to fix.
Carnegie's favorite proof of this comes from Abraham Lincoln, of all people, because Lincoln learned the lesson the hard way, twice.

As a young lawyer in Illinois, Lincoln liked to write mocking, anonymous letters to the local newspaper ridiculing his political opponents, and one target, a man named James Shields, eventually figured out Lincoln was behind a letter that had humiliated him in print.
Shields was furious enough to challenge Lincoln to a duel.
The two men actually met on a sandbar in the Mississippi River, broadswords in hand, prepared to fight, and it was only called off at the last minute by the men standing beside them.
Lincoln never forgot how close he'd come to dying over a cheap shot in a newspaper, and by every account, he never wrote another mocking word about anyone again.
That discipline was tested at the highest possible stakes years later, right after the Battle of Gettysburg.
Lee's army was retreating, but a flooded Potomac River had trapped them, they were sitting ducks, and Lincoln sent an urgent order to General Meade to attack immediately and finish the war.
Meade hesitated.
He called a council of war instead of moving.
By the time he finally advanced, the river had gone down and Lee's army had slipped across and escaped, dragging the war on for almost two more brutal years.

Lincoln was devastated, and he sat down and wrote Meade a letter that let him have it, telling him plainly that Lee had been within his easy grasp, that closing on him would have ended the war then and there, and that, in Lincoln's own words, your golden opportunity is gone, and I am distressed immeasurably because of it.
Read that line today and it still stings.
But Lincoln never sent the letter.
It was found among his papers after he died, still folded, never mailed.
Historians who've studied his habits think they know why.
Writing it let him blow off his own steam.

But mailing it wouldn't have brought back the lost chance to end the war, it would only have wounded a general who had, after all, just delivered the Union's biggest victory so far, made him defensive, maybe even provoked him to resign at the worst possible moment.
Lincoln kept asking himself something close to a question you should ask the next time you're furious at someone.
If I had been standing in that field, hearing the wounded screaming, watching exhausted men who'd already been fighting for days, would I have been so eager to charge again either?
Sitting safely behind a desk, it's always easy to demand courage from someone else.
And it isn't just a nice historical parable.
Decades later the psychologist B.F. Skinner ran experiment after experiment on animals and found something startlingly consistent.
An animal rewarded for good behavior learns dramatically faster, and holds onto what it learns far longer, than an animal punished for bad behavior.
Later research on humans found the same pattern.

Criticism might produce a flinch of short term compliance, but it doesn't produce lasting change, and what it reliably does produce is resentment, the kind that sits quietly under the surface and comes out sideways later.

The department store magnate John Wanamaker put it more simply after thirty years running his business, saying he'd learned long ago that it was foolish to scold, since he had enough trouble overcoming his own limitations without being upset that other people had limitations too.
So here's the technique, stated as plainly as Carnegie states it.

Before you criticize, condemn, or complain about anyone, anywhere, remember you're not dealing with creatures of logic, you're dealing with creatures of pride and ego and self justification, and every single one of them, like Crowley, like Capone, like Meade, already has a story in their head where they're the reasonable one.
Your criticism doesn't replace that story.
It just makes them defend it harder.
The second fundamental technique flips that same coin over.
If withholding criticism starves the fire, this next one is what actually feeds a person, and Carnegie is precise about the fuel.
He calls it honest, sincere appreciation, and he spends real time making sure you don't confuse it with its cheap imitation, flattery.
Flattery is telling someone what you think they want to hear, usually because you want something from them.
It's transactional, and most people, even when they enjoy it in the moment, can smell it.
Appreciation is different.
It's noticing something genuinely good in another person and saying it out loud, expecting nothing back.
One comes from your mouth.
The other comes from somewhere underneath your ribs.

The businessman Carnegie holds up as the master of this is Charles Schwab, who ran Andrew Carnegie's steel operation and later Bethlehem Steel, and who was, at one point, the highest paid executive in America, pulling in something close to a million dollars a year at a time when that was an almost unthinkable sum.
Schwab said the reason wasn't his knowledge of steelmaking.
He considered his ability to arouse enthusiasm in his people the greatest asset he possessed, and he believed there was nothing that killed a person's ambition faster than criticism from above.
In his own words, I am hearty in my approbation and lavish in my praise.
Sit with that phrase for a second, because it's basically the whole chapter in nine words.
He said he had never met a man, however high his station, who didn't do better work under a spirit of approval than he ever would under a spirit of criticism.
Then Carnegie gives you John D. Rockefeller Senior, a man not exactly famous for being soft, showing that same instinct in a moment that would have broken most bosses.
One of Rockefeller's partners, Edward Bedford, made a bad investment decision in South America that cost the company roughly a million dollars, the kind of mistake that ends careers.
Rockefeller could see that Bedford had genuinely done his best and that the damage was already done, so instead of tearing into him, he looked for something true to praise.
He pointed out that Bedford had actually managed to save sixty percent of the money that had been at risk.
That's splendid, Rockefeller told him.
We don't always do as well as that upstairs.
Notice the layers of self control packed into that sentence.
Rockefeller didn't pretend the mistake hadn't happened.
He simply chose to notice, out loud, the part of the story that was still genuinely praiseworthy, and in doing that, he kept a good executive from being crushed by one bad year.
Here's the discipline underneath both stories.
It isn't about pretending things are better than they are.
It's about training your eye to actually look for what's real and good in another person, then saying it, specifically and sincerely, instead of assuming they already know.
Most people go through entire years of their life starving for one honest sentence of appreciation from someone whose opinion they respect.
You have the power to be the person who finally says it.

The third fundamental technique is the one Carnegie says, if you master nothing else in this whole book, will still change your life, because it's the difference between pushing people and pulling them.
He opens the chapter with one of his own favorite lines, worth remembering the next time you're trying to convince anyone of anything.
He liked strawberries and cream, he said, but for some strange reason, fish prefer worms.
So when he went fishing, he didn't think about what he wanted, he thought about what the fish wanted, and baited the hook accordingly.
Why not use that same common sense when you're fishing for people?
Instead of standing there talking about what you want, the raise you deserve, the favor you need, the change you're pushing for, find the worm.
Find what the other person actually wants, and show them how getting it also gets you what you need.
The psychologist Harry Overstreet, whose work Carnegie leaned on heavily, put the whole principle in a single now famous idea.

Action springs out of what we fundamentally desire, he argued, and the best advice anyone can give a would be persuader, in business, at home, in politics, anywhere, is to first arouse in the other person an eager want, because the person who can do that has the whole world with him, and the person who can't walks a lonely road.

For a picture of what that looks like in practice, Carnegie goes back to the childhood of Andrew Carnegie, the Scottish immigrant who built the largest steel empire in the world, and who, despite sharing a last name with Dale Carnegie, was no relation to him at all.
As a boy in Scotland, young Andrew caught a mother rabbit, and before long he had a hutch full of baby rabbits and no food to feed them.
He also had a yard full of neighborhood boys who loved to play.
So he made them an offer.
If they'd go out and gather enough clover and dandelions to keep the rabbits fed, he would name a rabbit after each boy who helped.
It worked like magic.
Every boy in the neighborhood was suddenly desperate to bring in armfuls of clover, thrilled that a rabbit bore his name.

Andrew Carnegie never forgot that lesson, and he used it for the rest of his career on a much bigger scale, which we'll come back to shortly, because it's also the secret behind one of the most famous business mergers in American industrial history.
Owen D. Young, who chaired General Electric, is remembered making almost the same point in more grown up language.
A person who can put himself in another person's place, who can genuinely understand how their mind works, Young said, never has to worry much about what the future holds for him.
That's the whole skill, condensed.
Before you ask for anything, stop and ask what this looks like from the other side of the table.
What does this person actually want, fear, need, or dream about, and is there an honest way to connect what you're asking for to that?
Not manipulation, Carnegie is careful to say, this only works over the long run when the want you're pointing at is real and the benefit to the other person is genuine.

But when you do the work of finding the real worm instead of assuming everyone wants your strawberries and cream, people stop feeling handled and start feeling understood, and a person who feels understood will walk through walls for you.
That's part one.

Part two of the book is probably the most famous section, six specific, concrete ways to make people like you, and Carnegie insists every one of them can be practiced starting in your very next conversation.
The first way is to become genuinely interested in other people.
Carnegie opens with an image everybody already knows.
Think about a dog.
A dog doesn't have to sell you real estate or manage your accounts to make you love it.
All it has to do is be thrilled you walked through the door.
You can make more real friends in two months by becoming genuinely interested in other people, Carnegie says, than you can in two years trying to get other people interested in you.

The Austrian psychologist Alfred Adler backed this up from the clinical side, writing that it is the individual who is not interested in his fellow men who has the greatest difficulties in life and causes the greatest injury to others, and that it's from exactly this kind of person that human failures spring.

For a living example, Carnegie points to Theodore Roosevelt, who by every account had an almost supernatural gift for making anyone, from a foreign diplomat down to the kitchen help, feel like the most interesting person in the room.
His valet, James Amos, wrote an entire memoir about him, full of small moments like this.

Years after leaving the White House, Roosevelt came back for a visit while President Taft and his wife happened to be away, and he greeted every member of the household staff by name, down to the scullery maids, asking Alice in the kitchen if she still made her corn bread, genuinely disappointed to hear the upstairs family didn't eat it anymore.
Staff who had worked in that house for years under other presidents said nobody else had ever done that.
It cost Roosevelt nothing, and it made every person in that building feel seen.
The second way is disarmingly simple.
Smile.
Carnegie quotes an old Chinese proverb that says a man without a smiling face must not open a shop, then makes the business case for why that's just as true in New York as it was in old China.

He tells of the employment manager of a large New York department store who said flatly that she would rather hire a shop girl who never finished grade school but had a genuine, warm smile, than a philosophy professor with an impressive resume and a sour face.

That same department store printed a little card for its staff about the value of a smile, saying it costs nothing but creates everything, that it enriches the person who receives it without costing the person who gives it, that it happens in an instant but the memory of it can last forever, and that no one is so rich they don't need it and no one so poor they aren't made richer by it.
Here's the part that makes this more than a nice sentiment.
Carnegie leans on the psychologist William James's observation that action and feeling travel together, so while you can't always directly command a feeling, you can command the action around it.
If you've lost your natural cheerfulness, James argued, the way back is to sit up, act, and speak as though the cheerfulness is already there.
Force the smile, even on a rough day, even alone in your car before you walk into a meeting, and the feeling has a strange habit of following the muscle.
The third way is one of the most practical tools in the entire book.
Remember that a person's name is, to that person, the sweetest and most important sound in any language.
Carnegie's proof case here is Jim Farley, who ran Franklin Roosevelt's political campaigns and later became Postmaster General despite never setting foot inside a high school.
When Carnegie asked him about his legendary memory, Farley said he could call fifty thousand people by their first names.

He built that skill deliberately, starting back when he worked as a small town clerk, making a point of learning every new acquaintance's full name, family situation, line of work, and even political leanings, filing it all away so thoroughly he could greet the same person warmly a year later and ask about their sick uncle by name.
During the Roosevelt campaign, Farley wrote hundreds of personal letters a day, then physically traveled through twenty states in nineteen days, collecting names as he went.
That single skill, treating a name like it mattered, is a real part of what put a president in the White House.

And this is where Andrew Carnegie comes back into the story, because before he was known for libraries and steel mills, he was known in the industry as a man who understood exactly what a name was worth.

Early in his career, wanting the Pennsylvania Railroad's business for his steel rails, he built a massive new steel works and simply named the whole plant the Edgar Thomson Steel Works, after the railroad's president.
It isn't hard to guess whose rails the Pennsylvania Railroad decided to buy after that.

Years later, Carnegie was locked in a bruising price war with George Pullman over who would win the sleeping car contract for the Union Pacific Railroad, both companies slashing prices until there was no profit left for anyone.
The two men happened to run into each other one evening at a New York hotel, and Carnegie said something like, good evening, Mr. Pullman, aren't we making a couple of fools of ourselves?
He proposed merging their two companies instead of destroying each other.
Pullman was interested but not sold, and finally asked what they'd call the new company.
Carnegie answered instantly, why, the Pullman Palace Car Company, of course.
Pullman's whole face changed.
He invited Carnegie up to his room to talk it over properly, and that conversation reshaped the entire sleeping car industry.
Carnegie got half the business and all the peace, and it cost him nothing but another man's name on the door.
The fourth way is to become a genuinely good listener, and to encourage other people to talk about themselves.
Carnegie tells this one on himself.

At a dinner party in New York, he was introduced to a botanist, and having never really talked with one before, he found himself completely absorbed, asking question after question about exotic plants and experiments with ordinary potatoes, whatever the man wanted to talk about, for what turned into hours.
Carnegie barely said a word about himself all evening.
When he finally had to leave, the botanist went out of his way to tell the host that Carnegie was one of the most stimulating conversationalists he'd met in ages.
Carnegie's own verdict on himself, looking back, was that he had said almost nothing all night.
He had simply listened, with real attention, and asked questions that showed he was actually following along.
That was enough to earn a reputation as brilliant, without contributing a single interesting fact of his own.

Most people don't want a clever conversation partner nearly as much as they want someone who will let them think out loud about the thing they care about most, which happens to be, for almost everyone, themselves.
The fifth way is to talk in terms of the other person's interests, not your own.
Theodore Roosevelt shows up here again, because his staff noticed a habit of his.

Whenever he knew someone was coming to see him, Roosevelt would stay up late the night before reading up on whatever subject he knew that particular visitor cared most about, whether it was cattle ranching or naval history or rare birds.
It didn't matter to him whether he personally found the topic fascinating.
He understood something simple and enormously effective.
The fastest road into another person's goodwill runs directly through the thing they already love talking about.
Walk in ready to talk about their world instead of yours, and you've made yourself welcome before you've asked for anything at all.
And the sixth way, which Carnegie treats almost as the summary of everything before it, is to make the other person feel important, and to mean it.
He tells a small story on himself that sticks with people more than almost anything else in the book.

He was standing at a post office on Thirty Third Street in New York, waiting behind a registry clerk who was clearly bored out of his mind, weighing envelopes, stamping receipts, doing the same four motions over and over, year after year.
Carnegie looked at the young man for a second and said, genuinely, I certainly wish I had your head of hair.

The clerk looked up, startled, and broke into a real smile, a little embarrassed, saying it wasn't as good as it used to be, but clearly delighted someone had noticed anything about him at all that day.
Carnegie always figured that clerk walked out to lunch a little lighter, maybe mentioned it to his wife that night, maybe caught his own reflection and thought, not bad, actually.
It cost Carnegie four seconds and a moment of genuine attention.

He collected dozens of these small moments from his students too, information clerks complimented for answering a question clearly, telegraph operators, department clerks, delivery drivers, the whole invisible army of people who do repetitive service work and rarely hear a specific, sincere word about the part of their job they're actually good at.
None of these people needed flattery.
They needed someone to notice something true and say it out loud.

Everyone you pass today, the barista, the toll booth worker, the coworker who quietly reorganized the shared drive nobody asked them to fix, is carrying around some quiet competence nobody has mentioned in a while.
You can be the person who mentions it.
Part three of the book moves from how you treat people day to day into something harder, how you actually change someone's mind without turning the conversation into a fight.
The very first principle sets the tone for everything that follows.
The only way to get the best of an argument, Carnegie says, is to avoid it entirely.
He illustrates this with a story about an old friend of his named Frank Gammond, a man who had spent years studying Shakespeare.

The two of them were at a banquet together when another guest, midway through a toast, quoted the line there's a divinity that shapes our ends, rough hew them how we will, and confidently credited it to the Bible.
Carnegie knew for a fact the line was Shakespeare, from Hamlet, and so, sitting right next to him, did Gammond.
Carnegie turned to his old friend for backup, ready to correct the man in front of the whole table.

Instead, Gammond kicked him under the table, and once the toast was over, quietly told him that of course the line was from Hamlet, but they were guests at a festive dinner, nobody had asked for a correction, and proving a man wrong in front of a room full of people was never going to make him like you.
Why not let him save face, Gammond said.
He didn't ask for your opinion.
Why argue with him?
Then he added a line Carnegie never forgot, always avoid the acute angle.
Winning that argument would have cost Carnegie something far more valuable than being right for thirty seconds, and Gammond knew it.
That connects straight into the next two principles, which really work as a pair.
Show real respect for the other person's opinion and never flatly tell them they're wrong, and if you find out you're the one who's wrong, admit it quickly, openly, and without excuses.
Carnegie has a wonderful personal story about that second half.
He used to walk his little Boston bulldog, Rex, in a nearby park, off the leash and without a muzzle, technically against the rules, but the park was usually empty, so he kept taking the risk.
One day a mounted policeman stopped him and warned him that next time it would mean a trip in front of the judge.
Rex hated the muzzle as much as Carnegie did, so they kept taking their chances, until the day they ran into that same officer again.
This time, before the policeman could say a single word, Carnegie beat him to it.
Officer, he said, you've caught me red handed, I'm guilty, no excuses, no alibis.

Instead of writing the ticket, the policeman softened completely, said a little dog like that surely wasn't going to hurt anybody, and told him to just let the dog run where he couldn't be seen, and they'd forget the whole thing.
The fastest way to defuse someone who has every right to be angry at you is to admit the fault yourself, immediately and fully, before they even have to accuse you.
Once you've cleared the air that way, Carnegie says, begin every difficult conversation in a friendly tone, because a drop of honey really does catch more flies than a gallon of vinegar.
Then get the other person agreeing with you early, ideally saying yes to something small and true before you ever reach the part where you need them to agree with something bigger.

This is what he calls the secret of Socrates, after the philosopher's habit of asking questions his opponent could only answer yes to, gently walking them toward a conclusion they'd have fought if you had simply stated it outright.
He gives two real examples from his own students.

A bank teller named James Eberson had a customer refuse to fill out part of the bank's standard form, and instead of insisting it was mandatory, Eberson simply asked a few questions the man could only answer yes to, like whether he'd want the bank to send his money automatically to his next of kin if anything ever happened to him.
Once the man was agreeing, he filled out the rest of the form himself, willingly.

A Westinghouse salesman named Joseph Allison spent thirteen years, on and off, trying to sell motors to one particular customer, and when the man finally bought some and then complained they ran too hot to touch, Allison didn't argue.

He agreed a motor that hot would be a real problem, then walked the man through the accepted industry standard together, question by question, until the customer worked out for himself that the motor was actually running within spec.
He placed a bigger order right after.
That habit of asking instead of insisting leads naturally into one of the sneakiest and most useful principles in the whole section, letting the other person feel that the idea was theirs.
An illustrator named Eugene Wesson had been failing for months to sell his sketches to design buyers, showing up with finished work and getting rejected again and again.
So he changed his approach completely.
He started bringing in unfinished sketches and simply asking the buyers how they thought the pieces should be completed.
The buyers, now genuinely invested, gave him their ideas, he finished the work based on their input, and suddenly he couldn't stop selling.
The sketches hadn't gotten better.
The buyers just felt like part owners of them now.

Pair that with trying honestly to see the situation from the other person's point of view before you open your mouth, the same instinct Owen D. Young showed when giving instructions to his staff, phrasing everything as you might consider this or what do you think of this, rather than a flat command, and appealing to someone's nobler motives instead of threatening them.
Carnegie tells of a landlord with a tenant determined to break his lease four months early.
Instead of citing the contract or threatening legal action, the landlord simply told the man he believed him to be someone who kept his word, and said he'd accept whatever the man ultimately decided.
The tenant and his wife talked it over and came back to pay the rent in person, telling the landlord that the only honorable thing to do was live up to what they had signed.
As the financier J.P. Morgan once put it, a person usually has two reasons for doing anything, one that sounds good, and the real one.
Appeal to the good sounding one, the honorable one, and very often the person will supply the follow through themselves.
The last principle in this section is to dramatize your ideas, and when reasoning alone isn't landing, to throw down a challenge.
For that, Carnegie tells what might be the single most famous story in the entire book.

Charles Schwab was touring one of his steel mills that was chronically underperforming, and asked the day shift foreman how many heats, meaning how many melts of steel, his crew had completed that day.
Six, the foreman said.
Without a word of criticism, Schwab took a piece of chalk, wrote a big number six on the floor, and walked away.
When the night shift came in and asked what the six meant, they were told the big boss had asked about their production and chalked down the answer.
The night shift, stung by the implied comparison, worked harder and rubbed out the six, replacing it with a seven before they left.
The next morning the day shift saw the seven, got competitive right back, and by the end of that day had chalked up a ten before heading home.

A mill that had been quietly lagging behind every other plant in the company was, within a short stretch of time, out producing every one of them, not because Schwab lectured anybody about productivity, but because he turned a number into a contest nobody wanted to lose.
That brings us to the fourth and final section, which Carnegie calls being a leader, how to change people without giving offense or sparking resentment.
If you're a parent, a manager, a coach, or honestly just someone who occasionally has to give another adult difficult feedback, this is the part to replay.

The first move is to always begin with honest praise and appreciation before you say anything critical, because criticism that opens a conversation puts the other person on the defensive before you've even reached your actual point.
Then, when you do need to point out a mistake, do it indirectly rather than head on.
Schwab, once again, gives the model.
Walking through one of his mills, he passed a group of men smoking directly underneath a large No Smoking sign.
A lesser manager starts yelling.
Schwab walked over, handed each man a cigar, and said he'd appreciate it if they'd go smoke outdoors.
Every man in that group knew exactly what he meant, and every one of them respected him more for how he'd said it, not less.
Before you criticize anyone else, though, Carnegie insists you talk about your own mistakes first.
He learned this hiring his own niece, Josephine, fresh out of high school, as his secretary.

She made plenty of beginner's mistakes, and his first instinct was to correct her sharply, until he stopped and reminded himself that he was roughly twice her age and had years more experience, and that at nineteen he himself had made far worse blunders than anything she was making now.
So instead of criticizing outright, he'd bring up something clumsy he had done at her age before gently mentioning what she might try differently.
She took the feedback far better, because he'd equalized the ground between them before asking her to change.
From there, ask questions instead of issuing direct orders.
Owen D. Young, again, is the model, because he almost never told a subordinate flatly what to do.
He would say, you might consider this, or, what do you think of that, and after dictating a letter, he'd often ask a junior assistant, what do you think of this one?
That small shift saves a person's pride and turns them into a collaborator instead of someone just taking orders, which makes them want to cooperate rather than quietly resist.
And whenever you do need to make a real change, a demotion, a reassignment, a hard correction, let the other person keep their dignity intact.

General Electric once needed to remove the brilliant but famously difficult engineer Charles Steinmetz from running a department he wasn't suited to manage, without insulting a man the company considered irreplaceable.
So they created a new title for him, Consulting Engineer of General Electric, technically a lateral move for work he was largely doing already, and quietly installed someone else as department head.
Steinmetz was pleased.
The company got what it needed without a single blowup, because nobody had to lose face to get there.

Once someone starts to improve, even slightly, praise that improvement specifically, and keep praising every bit of further progress, the same way Schwab said he was hearty in his approval and lavish in his praise.
Give people, especially the ones you're hardest on, a fine reputation to live up to.
A fourth grade teacher once learned she was getting a notoriously difficult student named Tommy in her class that fall.
On his very first day, she told him she had heard he was a natural leader, and that she was counting on him to help make this the best class in the whole grade.
Tommy, who had never once been called a leader by an adult in his life, spent the rest of the year quietly trying to live up to that description.
It's the same instinct behind that landlord and his honorable tenant from the last section.
Tell people who they could be, sincerely, and a surprising number of them will grow into it.
Use encouragement, and make whatever needs fixing sound easy to correct, not like evidence of some deep flaw.
Carnegie was a terrible dancer, by his own admission, when he first started taking lessons as an adult, but his instructor refused to let him believe that.

She told him his sense of rhythm was actually quite good, that he simply hadn't had much practice yet, and that framing alone kept him showing up to lessons instead of quitting in embarrassment after the first week.
Tell someone a mistake is minor and fixable, and they'll believe you and go fix it.
Tell them it's a sign of some deeper failing, and they'll usually just stop trying in front of you.
And the whole section closes on one final idea that ties every principle in this book together.
Make the other person genuinely happy about doing the thing you're suggesting.
Not resigned to it, not guilted into it, happy.

Every technique we've walked through today, from remembering someone's name to admitting your own mistake first, is really just a different route to that same destination, a person who ends an interaction with you feeling more important, more respected, and more capable than they did when the conversation started.

So think back to where we began, a broke young man with no textbook, standing in front of a room of tired adults at a Harlem YMCA, forced to invent all of this from scratch because nothing like it existed yet.

Everything you just heard, the criminal who couldn't blame himself, the steel man who was lavish with his praise, the little boy naming rabbits after the neighbor kids, the policeman who let a guilty man go because he confessed before he was accused, all of it came out of index cards Carnegie filled over twenty years of watching ordinary adults succeed and fail with each other in that same kind of room.
That's why, ninety years and thirty million copies later, you can still feel it working.
It isn't theory.
It's a few hundred real interactions, examined closely enough to find the pattern underneath them.
And that pattern is almost embarrassingly consistent once you've heard it laid out end to end.

Every single principle in this book, whether it's holding your criticism, remembering a name, admitting a fault before you're caught, or handing someone a reputation to live up to, is doing exactly one thing.
It's making the other person feel like they matter, honestly and specifically, instead of like an obstacle standing between you and what you want.
Cialdini would later call parts of this liking and reciprocity.
Voss would later call parts of it tactical empathy.
Carnegie just called it the biggest single secret of dealing with people, and nearly everything written on the subject since has amounted to a footnote on his original idea.
So here's what to actually do with all of this, starting this week, because Carnegie built this whole system for people who wanted to practice, not just nod along in the car.
First, this week, go a full day without criticizing, condemning, or complaining about a single person, out loud or in your own head, and notice how hard that actually is.

When you catch yourself about to point out what someone did wrong, pause and ask what story they're probably telling themselves about why they did it, the way Crowley and Capone had their own stories, and see if that changes what you say next.

Second, find one person you see almost every day and haven't specifically appreciated in a while, a coworker, your partner, your kid, and give them one honest, specific sentence of praise, not a generic nice job, but the exact thing you noticed, the way Schwab was hearty in his approbation and lavish in his praise.
Do it before the week is out.
Third, in your next real conversation with someone you need something from, spend the first few minutes finding out what they want before you say a word about what you want.
Ask a question.
Actually listen to the answer.
Then look for the honest overlap between their want and yours, the worm, not the strawberries and cream.
Fourth, pick one person whose name you've been getting slightly wrong, or forgetting, or just not using, and use their full name warmly the next time you see them.
Notice their face change.
That's the whole Jim Farley trick, and it costs you nothing but attention.

Fifth, the next time you feel an argument starting to build, whether it's with a spouse, a friend, or someone online, remember Frank Gammond kicking Carnegie under the table, and ask yourself whether winning this one is actually worth what it will cost you.
If you're the one in the wrong, say so immediately and fully, before you're accused, the way Carnegie did with that policeman over his dog.
It disarms people almost every time.

Sixth, at work this week, take one instruction you were about to hand someone as a flat order and rephrase it as a question instead, something like, what do you think about trying it this way, the way Owen D. Young did with his staff.
Watch whether the person's whole posture toward the task changes.
And seventh, find one person, maybe your kid, maybe someone new on your team, who could use a fine reputation to live up to, and give them one, sincerely, this week.
Tell them you've noticed they're the reliable one, or the thoughtful one, or the one who always follows through, even if it's a little aspirational right now.
Then watch what happens over the next few weeks, because more often than anyone expects, people grow directly into the description you hand them.
None of this requires you to become a different person.
Carnegie's entire body of work rests on the idea that you already know how to be warm, because you're warm with the people you love without even trying.
The whole task is just remembering to extend that same attention to everyone else, on purpose, starting with the very next person you talk to when this ends.
